differential_diagnoses: Infectious etiology with atypical presentation (e.g., fungal or mycobacterial)
next_steps: Secure airway given proximity of lesion to the oropharynx
Perform diagnostic excisional biopsy of the peritonsillar mass with ENT/Head and Neck surgery
Immediate histopathological evaluation with H&E staining
Immunohistochemistry including desmin, MyoD1, myogenin, and pancytokeratin
Molecular testing including FISH for FOXO1 and NGS for sarcoma-associated mutations
Contrast-enhanced MRI or MRA to evaluate for vascular involvement or pseudoaneurysm
Interdisciplinary consultation with oncology and pathology
Perform immediate contrast-enhanced CT angiography (CTA) or MRA of the neck to evaluate for vascular involvement, specifically internal carotid artery pseudoaneurysm
Consult interventional radiology for potential endovascular management or coil embolization if a vascular lesion is confirmed
Ensure vascular surgery is on standby if further surgical exploration or biopsy is required
Maintain patient in a hospital setting with strict precautions against further tissue manipulation until vascular involvement is definitively excluded
Perform diagnostic biopsy only after ruling out a vascular origin for the mass, ensuring it is done under general anesthesia in an operating room with full hemostatic control
Obtain tissue for comprehensive histopathology, including H&E, IHC (Desmin, MyoD1, myogenin, CD56, EBER), and molecular testing for sarcoma-associated mutations
